content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
December 10,
1992
Torn Parks, et al.
4907 East Hatch Road
Hughson,
CA
95326
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—92—63l
Dear Mr. Parks:
You have requested advice under the campaign disclosure
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”) •1
You have asked if there are reporting requirements under the
Political Reform Act for a group of individuals that spent $276 on
a letter opposing a ballot measure.
The Act provides that “committees” must file campaign
disclosure statements at specified intervals.
(Sections 82013;
84100,
Section 82013 defines “committee” as any person or conibina—
tion of persons who directly or indirectly does any of the follow
ing:
(a)
Receives contributions totaling one
thousand dollars
($1,000) or more in a calendar
year; or
V
(b)
Makes independent expenditures totaling
one thousand dollars
($1,000) or more in a calendar
year; or
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000,
.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807
•
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Tom Parks
A—9 2—631
Page
2
(C)
Makes contributions totaling ten thousand
dollars
($10,000)
or more in a calendar year to or
at the behest of candidates or committees.
Based on the information you have provided concerning the
financial activity of the individuals, the group did not qualify
as a committee and is not required to file a campaign disclosure
statement.
If you have further questions concerning this letter, please
contact me at
(916)
322—5662.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
I
L_-/
Iii
c’d’24 i2
-t2c-’
By:
Sandra L. Silva
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance Division

sections.question: You have asked if there are reporting requirements under the Political Reform Act for a group of individuals that spent $276 on a letter opposing a ballot measure.
sections.conclusion: Based on the information you have provided concerning the financial activity of the individuals, the group did not qualify as a committee and is not required to file a campaign disclosure statement.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a group of individuals that spent $276 on a letter opposing a ballot measure must file campaign disclosure statements under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The group does not qualify as a 'committee' under the Act because their expenditure of $276 does not meet the $1,000 threshold for independent expenditures, and therefore no campaign disclosure statement is required.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Standard advice letter format with clear question and conclusion sections. Document provides statutory framework (Government Code Section 82013) defining 'committee' and applies it to the requestor's factual situation.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You have asked if there are reporting requirements under the Political Reform Act for a group of individuals that spent $276 on a letter opposing a ballot measure.

CONCLUSION: Based on the information you have provided concerning the financial activity of the individuals, the group did not qualify as a committee and is not required to file a campaign disclosure statement.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises Tom Parks and others that a group spending $276 on a letter opposing a ballot measure is not required to file campaign disclosure statements because the expenditure falls below the $1,000 threshold that would classify them as a committee under the Political Reform Act.
